data.title: Macro Insights From September 2025: A Government Shutdown, The Fed's Gamble, And A Bubble Ready To Burst
data.description:
Introduction

Welcome to the SA’s The Macro Brief! This official SA Profile will highlight our analysts' latest economic and market analysis to help investors gauge the ever-volatile financial landscape through various recurring series.

The economy is the foundation of financial markets, influencing everything from corporate earnings and consumer spending to central bank policy and inflation. Understanding economic trends, policy decisions, and sector activity can be critical when assessing market opportunities and making informed investment decisions.

Editor's Note: Starting in October, the Macro Insights series will be published twice a month. This increased frequency will provide more timely analysis and expert commentary, ensuring you stay on top of the most critical macroeconomic events and trends.

Check out these must-reads from September…but first, a few bonus articles about the U.S. government shutdown...

September’s Trending Themes

A Government Shutdown

The U.S. Government Shutdown Is Looming | Danil Kolyako | 9/25/25

“At the same time, I think any shutdown-related market weakness should be used as a buying opportunity for investors, so I keep a constructive view of the U.S. stock market.”

The Impact On The Economy And Markets Of The Looming Government Shutdown | Michael Gray | 9/26/25

“Historically, Government Shutdowns have not had a negative impact on stocks, as the S&P 500 Index has gained an average of 5.5% over the ensuing 40-day period following the shutdown.”

The Fed’s Gamble

The Fed Could Break The Market | Victor Dergunov | 9/15/25

“Despite labor market softness, recession risk remains low, and corporate profitability is buoyed by AI, efficiency gains, and positive outlooks.”

Since 1980, This Is How Rate Cuts Impact The S&P 500 Near All-Time Highs | Multiplo Invest | 9/15/25

“Historical data shows the S&P 500 typically rises after the Fed cuts near all-time highs, supporting a 12-month target of 7247 points.”

The Fed May Be Poised To Make A Major Mistake | Samuel Smith | 9/17/25

“In particular, the Fed's decision will be difficult not only this month but in the months to come because it appears to be playing a game of hot potato right now…”

Why I'm Positioning My Portfolio For The Fed's Most Dangerous Gamble Yet | Leo Nelissen | 9/21/25

“I see a paradigm shift as the Fed and White House risk higher inflation to fuel growth, creating both huge risks and massive opportunities.”

Why This Fed Rate Cut Is Terrible News For Some Dividend Stocks | High Yield Investor | 9/23/25

“Rate cuts may look bullish, but certain sectors could face painful losses. Find out which ones in particular may not enjoy the rally investors are expecting.”

Fed Rate Cut: Powell Pushes Back | Trapping Value | 9/23/25

“The Federal Reserve's recent 25 basis point rate cut has had limited impact on long-term Treasury yields and mortgage rates, challenging policy effectiveness.”

A Bubble Ready To Burst?

Are We Currently In A Market Bubble Like The One In 1999-2000? | Bill Gunderson | 9/3/25

“Momentum and robust earnings have kept markets at record highs, but the risk-to-reward ratio is now thin, and momentum could shift quickly.”

I'm Raising Cash: 5 Warning Signs Of A Market Bubble Are Here | Hawkinvest | 9/19/25

“The S&P 500 is at all-time highs, but warning signs suggest the market is overbought and vulnerable to a correction.”

The Market Setup Has Rarely Been This Treacherous | Mott Capital Management | 9/21/25

“The S&P 500 has reached some extremely overbought conditions in the last couple of days. On top of that, volatility has compressed, and liquidity has drained. This makes for a dangerous setup rather than the bullish view that investors have become so comfortable with.”

Why I Believe The Market Rally Is Losing Steam | Mikhail Fedorov | 9/23/25

“A two-speed economy has emerged: Tech and AI sectors surge, while broader lending and consumer demand stagnate under high interest rates.”

Why The Correction Is Starting | Victor Dergunov | 9/24/25

“The recent market rebound has driven the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 sharply higher, but extreme greed signals caution.”

Why Stocks Haven't Crashed (Yet?) | James Foord | 9/25/25

“Markets continue to reach new highs despite recession signals, driven by government intervention, monetary debasement, passive flows, and big tech leadership.”

It Is Like 1999 All Over Again | Bret Jensen | 9/28/25

“The market is rising even as economic warning signs increase, including the deteriorating jobs and housing markets, reminiscent of past bubbles.”

A Generational Opportunity To Buy Dividends At Fire-Sale Prices | Samuel Smith | 9/28/25

“While there remains a lot of uncertainty in the economy right now and evidence suggests that the labor market and consumer confidence are weakening, GDP numbers remain quite strong.”

I'm Starting To Believe AI Is A Bubble: The Curious Case Of OpenAI | Eugenio Catone | 9/30/25

“Current valuations seem driven by AI hype rather than fundamentals, with the market overlooking the systemic risks of OpenAI's unsustainable cash burn.”

Conclusion

We hope our analysts' insights serve as a valuable financial compass for investors seeking to make informed investment decisions.

Thanks for reading. Tell us what you think by commenting below! If you'd like to see more, follow this account.

Please let us know if you have any suggestions you’d like to see added to this series and any ideas for new additional recurring series. We're always open to your valuable feedback.

To stay current on the latest economic and market events coming up, subscribe to Wall Street Breakfast, Seeking Alpha's flagship daily business news summary.

Disclaimer: Written by SA Editors

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Growth Acceleration Again
data.description:
Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE:EPD) took some time to pick up a small acquisition that improves its position in the Midland Basin. The builds upon the value story of the last article. That purchase closed last month. So, it will be part of the third quarter report. The expected return on this acquisition is in the mid-teens (percentage). Both sides of this deal were knowledgeable players, so this deal likely was made with the requirements of both sides in mind. Nonetheless, this deal has benefits that go beyond the initial purchase and returns estimates.

The biggest "extra" is that assets in use (as in used assets) can often be purchased at a discount to building those assets. That means the deal usually starts with unusually high profitability, as noted above, before any "improvements" are made.

Oftentimes, a company like Occidental Petroleum (OXY) sold these assets because they were not being used to the fullest extent. Profitability was likely subpar compared to elsewhere. In many ways, this was a benefit to Occidental because it got rid of an asset not nearly as profitable as assets located elsewhere.

This often happens because plans change in the fast-changing commodity business. Indeed, Enterprise Products notes that there are several potential profit moves to be made. That is a roundabout way of stating this asset will be used far more by Enterprise Products than it was by Occidental. As a result, there may be some obvious moves to increase ROIC from what was already stated as part of the deal.

But buying assets on the cheap is a nice way to lower corporate breakeven points while increasing profitability. A company, for example, that can buy assets for $300 million is going to be more profitable than a company that builds those same assets for $500 million. Enterprise Products has long been a very profitable partnership. The example above is part of the reason why.

Next is the fact that debottlenecking and other cheap add-on projects are still available to an asset purchased like this. Due to their fast paybacks, they often increase profitability still more over the original asset profitability projection.

That means a small acquisition like this can have profit implications over the next few years not originally envisioned in the initial returns case.

Again, the acquisition begins with an ROIC return that is likely around 15% (give or take). But this is above what is shown for the whole company. That initial projection provides a small margin of safety against a disappointing return. The initial return could be below projections but fit right in with what the company is doing.

Cash Flow Growth

Despite the cyclical nature of the upstream business, the midstream business is much less volatile.

Some of the downside resistance comes from the fact that there is a natural lag between when the customers go shopping for more capacity and when that capacity comes online. As a result, during any upstream downturn, there are nearly always pipelines coming into service (with take-or-pay minimum payments) that provide more income even as volumes in existing pipelines and equipment decline.

The interesting thing with this industry is that the first year of a recovery shows a sizable jump in earnings (usually) because of the idle capacity that becomes used to increase earnings (including the new projects that came online during the downturn).

This is likely to remain true with the threat of a current downturn. The company has projects that take more than a year to complete. Therefore, customers have to see that the capacity is needed, recession or not.

Distribution Growth

Above-average profitability allows for some cash retention to fund capital projects and protect distribution growth. Above-average profitability also means there is less need for debt to fund good returns.

A partnership like this one has a superior history by maintaining above-average profitability. The last article covers this strategy as well. This differs from many investors that believe returns can be amplified (in a positive direction) through the use of debt. Yet those companies with a long-term record of using debt are few and far between. Most superior returns, like what is shown above, happen from the superior profitability of the projects.

This company maintains an "A" grade debt rating. That may be about the highest rating I can find in the midstream industry. Such a debt rating is a tribute to the profitability of the projects in the company portfolio.

The current debt ratio is roughly 3.1. This is well below the typical debt ratio in the midstream industry. It is far closer to the ratios reported by captive midstream companies that often have one customer and are effectively controlled by that customer.

Summary

Enterprise Products Partners had made an acquisition that will likely add a couple of percentage points to the long-term returns of the common units. This augments the long history of growing the returns to shareholders. It also continues the strong buy idea (that issues a K-1) as a growth and income investment.

The combined long-term return is likely to be in the teens for the foreseeable future. The very well protected distribution is currently nearly 7%. This is slightly below what many investors report for long-term total returns.

What is interesting about that is the relatively high yield on the common despite the low payout ratio. But that means there may be some appreciation potential as the market recognizes that there are more returns in terms of generated cash than are indicated by the distribution.

Those other returns include stock buybacks and, of course, growth projects that increase the future profits of the company. Some cash also pays back debt to, in effect, create some financial flexibility to finance potential acquisitions like the current acquisition.

That means that management will keep the debt ratio below the goal so that a potential acquisition does not drive the debt ratio over the goal (as a general rule).

Risks

There is always the risk that a family-run organization like this will not be as well run by the next generation. Now, the location of the business and the inherent advantages already in place for the pipelines and customers serviced mean it would take some time for inferior management to become apparent. But it can happen.

The current political uncertainty with the government shutdown combined with the threat of tariffs and trade wars already has the market looking forward to a recession. But unless the current economic path reverses soon, we could be in for much worse.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PTY: My Favorite PIMCO CEF For Retirement Income
data.description:
PIMCO Corporate and Income Opportunity Fund (NYSE:PTY) has been my one of my favorite PIMCO CEF vehicles since May, this year when I circulated a piece titled: PTY: 10% Yielding CEF That Needs To Be In Your Portfolio.

For me, PTY was competing with PIMCO Access Income Fund (PAXS) in terms of which of these could claim Top 1 PIMCO spot in my income-oriented portfolio. The reason why I have been bullish on PAXS since January, 2024 was related to PAXS's superior distribution coverage - i.e., having sufficient net investment income generation in place to avoid material reliance on return-on-capital or realized gain components. So far, PAXS has delivered ~33% in total returns, outperforming PTY by roughly 10% over this time period.

Now, considering the prevailing market conditions and looking deeper into PTY's current offering, I have to say that PTY has indeed become my Top 1 pick not only within PIMCO CEF space, but also in the broader context of other fixed income tilted CEFs.

Let me explain.

Thesis review

I will start first with some negatives.

Yield-wise, PTY is one of the least attractive PIMCO CEFs. Currently, it offers a distribution yield of 9.8%, which is, for example, below other well-known alternatives such as PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) and PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) that yield 10.9% and 13.4%, respectively.

From the premium perspective, PTY is relatively richly priced. The current premium is 22% over NAV, which is above what PDO and PDI have - 6.2% and 16.5%, respectively.

Optically, this is a very bad setup for going long PTY.

However, I have a different view.

Speaking of yield issue, then I hope that you will agree with me that we are not talking about tangible difference here. Apart from PDI, which could be considered an outlier, PTY's yield is rather competitive. Importantly, a distribution yield of almost 10% is something that definitely moves the needle for most income investors out there.

The premium aspect is a but more complex. And this is where we have to appreciate PTY's strengths.

First, PTY carries a very favorable investment maturity profile, where the bulk of exposure is linked to short to medium-term maturities. The 0 - 1 year segment accounts for more than half of the total portfolio (as we can see in the table below).

It is a significant advantage in the current interest rate environment. Or let me correct myself, this kind of maturity profile comes in handy when we have a downwards sloping interest rate curve. We can, obviously, debate about the pace and magnitude of incremental interest rate cuts, but the overarching baseline is still that sooner or later the Fed will continue lowering the base rates. And it is exactly the near to medium-term fixed income segment that can benefit from this (i.e., duration-driven returns). The farther we go in the maturity line, the lower the impact from what the Fed does at the front-end of the curve. The recent interest rate cuts (actually, starting from mid 2024) show a great example - i.e., short-term rates going down, while long-term rates remain somewhat stable.

Second, even though this point is tightly correlated to the previous one, it is quite critical; hence I have decided to separate it. Namely, if we inject the presence of external leverage into PTY's maturity profile, then the effective weighted average maturity shrinks to 3.62x. What does it mean? Well, it makes the whole argument that I outlined in point 1 much stronger. So, because of the external leverage, PTY is even better positioned to reap the benefit from falling base rates.

Third, speaking of leverage, PTY is unique, especially within the PIMCO CEF space. Usually, CEFs carry an external leverage anywhere between 25% to 45% (even though their maximum limits are higher). The external leverage is necessary to enhance the underlying core yield that stems from direct fixed-income investments (e.g., the case for PDI's abnormal yield). At the same time, we have to understand that the higher the leverage, the higher the financial risks and the lower the incremental growth capacity.

So, why is PTY is unique? Because of the fact that it has only ~12.5% of its total asset base fueled by external leverage. It means that the PTY is more defensive than an average fixed-income CEF and it has stronger NII growth potential (from additional leverage). This is one of the key drivers for PTY's premium.

Fourth, even though PTY has so conservative leverage profile, looking at the UNII reports, we can see that the current distribution yield is almost entirely covered by the NII generation (92% on a YTD basis; the rest is realized gains and ROC). Given this kind of leverage, one would expect weak coverage.

The bottom line

In my humble opinion, the investment case for PTY has strengthened over the past couple of months.

The interest rates have come down and the future trend is with a clear negative slope, which should facilitate PTY security price expansion in the 0 - 5 year maturity segment. Plus, lower base rates should drive down the financing costs, thereby further boosting the distribution coverage.

Finally, the conservative leverage profile remains as huge asset in terms of keeping the potential downside risk mitigated (critical in the context of weakening economy).

PTY is my favorite PIMCO CEF.

data.title: The Investment Landscape Of Data Centers: Opportunities And Challenges
data.description:
By Sebastian Dooley, CFA, Senior Fund Manager

The digital age has ushered in a paradigm shift in how businesses operate and individuals engage with technology. Data centers play a central role in this transformation and have emerged as critical infrastructure for the modern economy. For investors, the story today revolves around a simple tension: accelerating demand versus constrained supply. Exploring the investment opportunities for data centers, the factors driving demand, the sector's challenges, and the European market's unique positioning reveals a complex yet promising landscape.

Why data centers are attractive investment opportunities today

Fundamental growth drivers

Data centers, the backbone of the digital ecosystem, have become increasingly attractive investment opportunities as the world’s reliance on technology has expanded dramatically. Cloud computing first drove demand for robust data storage, spurring a surge in data center construction. More recently, the rise of artificial intelligence has intensified pressure on firms to boost digital efficiency, fueling demand for advanced data-processing capabilities. As a result, global data center development is growing rapidly, and investors are following suit, attracted by assets offering durable income streams and leases with some of the world’s largest, AAA-rated corporations.

Factors driving increased demand

Several key factors are fueling the growing demand for data centers:

  1. Corporate needs: Large corporations have historically sought to house their computing needs in proprietary data centers or through co-location facilities. This trend continues to evolve as the demand for computing power increases.
  2. The cloud: The rise of cloud computing has fundamentally changed the landscape. Contrary to the perception of the cloud as an abstract entity, it comprises physical data centers strategically located to optimize data processing and storage. Over the last decade, data centers have proliferated in specific regions to support cloud services.
  3. Artificial intelligence (AI): The AI revolution has added another layer of complexity and demand. Companies are developing AI algorithms and seeking infrastructure to support the inference stage, where AI models are applied in real-world scenarios. This has led to a significant uptick in the demand for data center space, particularly in regions where cloud data centers are concentrated, allowing for easy access to underlying data.

The investment implications of supply constraints

While demand for data centers is rising, several factors limit supply. The availability of land, particularly in urban areas where data centers are often located, presents a significant challenge. Additionally, power constraints are becoming increasingly relevant. Since data centers require substantial amounts of electricity, sourcing and transmitting power to these facilities have become pressing issues.

Local government regulations are also evolving, with permitting processes becoming more stringent, further complicating the development of new data centers. For investors, these constraints present unique opportunities. The difficulty in developing new sites creates a scarcity of assets, enhancing the value of existing data centers. As demand continues to outstrip supply, rental growth and asset valuation in this sector are expected to remain strong.

Energy and infrastructure challenges facing the data center industry

Energy supply and management are crucial considerations for data centers. These facilities require massive and reliable electricity loads, yet the infrastructure to deliver that power often lags behind demand. Clusters of data centers in certain regions can create local bottlenecks: even when power generation is sufficient, transmission capacity often has not kept pace with the growing demand for power in these areas. As such, necessary public infrastructure upgrades can delay the development of new data centers.

Sustainability adds another layer of complexity. Regulators and investors alike are pushing for greener operations, and data centers are under pressure to source renewable energy. Many operators pursue power-purchase agreements or carbon offsets to meet these expectations. In practice, however, most facilities still draw directly from the grid, where renewable power is blended with fossil fuel sources. The challenge, however, is that renewable energy resources are unreliable power sources, and the need for gas, nuclear, or even coal-fueled base power needs can entangle power projects in environmental red tape that can delay uninterrupted energy, a requirement for data centers. These dynamics are likely to make innovations such as grid-scale battery storage and infrastructure modernization critical for long-term growth.

The unique opportunity of the European market

Nowhere are these challenges more visible than in Europe. Demand for capacity continues to surge, yet supply is constrained by limited land availability, strained grids, and evolving regulation. Urban expansion is making it increasingly difficult to secure suitable sites, while permitting and environmental reviews have lengthened development timelines.

Land availability: Finding suitable locations for new data centers is increasingly difficult, as urban development continues to encroach on potential sites.

Power supply constraints: The availability of reliable power sources is becoming a critical consideration in data center development. The government in the Netherlands has twice imposed moratoriums on the establishment of new data centers to better assess power requirements and grid capacity. These pauses underscore the scarcity of viable sites and the premium attached to projects with secured access to reliable electricity.

Geopolitical factors: The current geopolitical climate is driving nations to prioritize data sovereignty and security, especially regarding cloud storage facilities. European governments increasingly advocate for data storage and processing to remain within national boundaries, further fueling the demand for data center localization.

For investors, Europe illustrates the sector’s dual nature: heavy constraints that complicate new supply, but also scarcity value that enhances the appeal of existing facilities and well-sited projects.

Evolving dynamics in the data center market

Although the global outlook for the data center market remains positive, facilities built for generative AI tend to be far more speculative than those supporting cloud and AI inference activities. Inference - the use of trained models to perform real-world tasks like image recognition, language translation, or fraud detection - demands substantial computational resources.

Cloud and AI inference data centers address established, durable demand. In contrast, data centers associated with generative AI are primarily driven by forecasts of future AI adoption and are likely a key source of today’s near-term oversupply. Still, overall demand for data centers is expected to persist and intensify over the coming years, and the combination of robust demand and constrained supply will likely lead to continued rental growth and increased asset valuations.

One emerging trend is the shift toward edge computing, where data centers are placed closer to end-users to reduce latency and enhance performance. This trend presents both opportunities and challenges. While it allows for greater responsiveness and efficiency, it also complicates sourcing appropriate sites due to urban density, existing infrastructure, and reliable power. The potential to identify and retrofit existing assets to meet the requirements of edge computing presents a unique investment opportunity in a rapidly changing landscape.

As capital requirements for data centers continue to grow, investors are likely to increase their allocations to this sector. The evolving landscape will likely see data centers become a more mainstream component of investment portfolios, appealing to a broader range of investors seeking stable returns.

Data centers are essential assets in a digital economy

Data centers are at the heart of the digital economy, and their importance will only grow as corporate needs, cloud computing, and AI drive relentless demand. For investors, the opportunity lies in scarcity value: data center facilities that can secure reliable power and scale with the demand for AI will command a premium. At the same time, supply constraints, energy management, and regulatory hurdles present challenges that only skilled managers can navigate effectively. In the near term, a wave of announced expansions risks temporary oversupply, but such dislocations can create attractive entry points for active investors. Over the long term, data centers are poised to become a core allocation in portfolios - offering stability, growth, and exposure to one of the most critical infrastructures of the digital age.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CoreWeave Or Nebius? I'd Pick This One To Outperform
data.description:
Which One to Pick?

I covered both CoreWeave, Inc. (NASDAQ:CRWV) and Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS) in the past, and I'm happy to see both of my investment coverage histories shaping up the way I exactly hoped for.

As both NBIS and CRWV are operating in the neocloud business, it has become a very popular topic to compare them to each other in order to define the ultimate bet for the long run.

The "long run" is a questionable term that needs to be defined in the first place. If we think of 5-10 years ahead, then probably Nebius Group seems better because it's a "Group". I mean, it's not just the neocloud AI-Infrastructure-as-a-Service business shaping its prospects. With the core AI platform that has gained traction in recent weeks, Nebius owns Avride (autonomous cars for ride-hailing services and logistics), TripleTen (ed-tech platform with bootcamps and MOOC-format training programs), and also holds stakes in Toloka (AI development) and ClickHouse (open-source database management system). So, Nebius has indeed built a great-looking tech ecosystem, as my SA colleague, Sarfatti Investment Research, noted in their recent take, with a variety of different driving forces for its consolidated TAM.

Given all the above distinctions, picking the "long-term" winner becomes a straightforward process for most investors - bet on NBIS and forget about it; go check in 5-10 years.

But what many people fail to mention is that betting on CoreWeave in today's environment becomes a safer investment decision (at least in the medium term). And I have a few reasons to think so.

1. Specialization Means "Purer Play"

As of today, CoreWeave operates over 250,000 NVIDIA GPUs across more than 30 data centers, and it's specialized AI cloud infrastructure (unlike Nebius). The whole infrastructure they operate is purpose-built for accelerated computing, and their ecosystem (if I dare to call it so) is simpler to understand and value compared to Nebius' group of companies.

CoreWeave generates revenues through per-GPU-per-hour rental fees, providing such a service primarily to Microsoft (MSFT) and, to a smaller extent, to small IT start-ups. The heavy reliance on MSFT made CRWV look quite vulnerable, and when MSFT decided to partner with NBIS in early September (likely to diversify away third-party risks for Azure), some initial CRWV investors started to sell and shift their capital towards NBIS. They were likely thinking that CRWV is going to lose its prospective capacity from MSFT. But in reality, it looks like nobody's going to refuse using CRWV's services because it has managed to gain sufficient scale to retain basically all its long-term customers/users. And instead of seeing CoreWeave losing its partnership with Microsoft, or deal cuts, the opposite thing happened to it: Meta (META) signed a similar-sized deal with CRWV akin to the one signed between NBIS and MSFT less than a month ago.

CoreWeave shares popped 8.7% in premarket trading on Tuesday amid a new $14.2B deal with Meta Platforms for artificial intelligence computing power.

Under terms of the deal, which were first reported by Bloomberg, CoreWeave will provide the Mark Zuckerberg-led company with access to Nvidia's (NVDA) latest GB300 comments. “They loved our infrastructure in earlier contracts and came back for more,” Chief Executive Officer Michael Intrator told the news outlet.

Source: SA News

I believe that specialization in the AI-Infrastructure-as-a-Service business helps CoreWeave stay on the front line and be the "Nvidia of its niche," or the ultimate "go-to-GPU-provider," whatever we call it, when it comes to providing needed GPU capacity to its clients.

Basically, CoreWeave is a "purer play" in what both CRWV and Nebius Group do at their core, and while it's usually a less sustainable feature when the business cycle of the niche turns down, before it does, this peculiarity during up-cycles usually spells "higher growth rates" and "higher chances for success." That's just my understanding, but if one is still looking for a pure-play AIaaS stock in today's environment, then CRWV seems to be more tempting than anything else.

2. Scale and Backlog

CoreWeave holds a massive $30 billion backlog, which is 6 times bigger than the consensus FY2025 sales, according to another Seeking Alpha analyst, Yiannis Zourmpanos, who also wrote that CRWV's long-term structured contracts "reduce volatility, create sticky client relationships, and differentiate CoreWeave from spot-pricing, usage-based cloud competitors." I can't agree more on that.

And I think that CoreWeave's backlog will only get bigger over time because of one crucial thing - its close ties to Nvidia. Yes, Nebius also has close ties to Nvidia after the strategic equity financing round held last year, but CoreWeave is a long-dated NVDA partner, which states to be "the first cloud provider to deploy Nvidia's latest AI chips," which is a very important distinction.

So, again, that $30 billion in backlog is only the beginning for CoreWeave, and while I'm not trying to belittle Nebius Group's opportunity on the same front, CoreWeave clearly wins in terms of scale. And that scale enables CRWV to negotiate better terms with suppliers. Keeping in mind its ability to deploy the latest Nvidia chips before competitors, I think CRWV's advantage is actually long-lasting in nature.

CRWV started off earlier, and they should break even in terms of EPS much faster than Nebius. And that's the current Wall Street consensus, which I completely agree with.

And here we come to the third reason.

3. Valuation Gives CRWV More Room to Run vs. NBIS

I don't usually put too much emphasis on valuation when comparing 2 firms, when one is basically lagging in terms of its business cycle by 1-2 years (I'm talking about Nebius here). But it's important to know here that CoreWeave is trading at a steep discount to Nebius while having similar business growth rates.

Again, it's not really an apples-to-apples comparison, and we should also keep in mind that Nebius's growth rates before 2024 were coming from Yandex's corporate development. Anyway, that 462.2% YoY in sales is less than CRWV's 736.64% YoY growth for the same period.

A lot of the current valuation is explained by the forward growth rates (probably forward rates are the only metrics that define growth stocks' valuations), so let's have a look beyond just FY2024 and TTM figures.

Nebius is projected to reach $4.246 billion in sales by the end of its fiscal 2027, and it saw a massive positive earnings revision lately, which basically increased the previous projection by a factor of 2.

Next year, Nebius Group's sales should rise by ~192%, and then in 2027, the increase will be ~153% YoY. The top line is expanding, but the pace is slowing down (albeit by a little). At the same time, CoreWeave is projected to show a ~131% YoY increase next year and only a 49% YoY growth in 2027. So, in relative terms, NBIS is beating CRWV.

But in absolute terms, CRWV revenue is already beating Nebius' prospective FY2027 sales in size.

For their fiscal 2026, both companies' revenue growth looks almost identical in relative terms. Yes, NBIS beats CRWV here, but not as strongly as in 2027. But investors pay almost 3x the price of CRWV when they decide to buy NBIS as their ultimate pick in this lucrative niche.

I think it's not fair, and if I had to decide which of these 2 to pick in today's environment, I'd definitely go with CRWV, at least for the next 12 months.

Final Thoughts

I should note here that I still rate NBIS as "Buy" because its long-term prospects of monetizing the widespread tech ecosystem look massive indeed. But again, CoreWeave is a smarter and safer play for the medium term for all the reasons I listed above, and it also receives a "Buy" from me today.

Both companies are exposed to a lot of risk factors, though, and it's important to understand what you're buying firsthand. The whole bullish thesis on AIaaS is based on the rising need for GPU capacity and wider AI agent usage in different applications. It's becoming a well-known expectation these days, but if we all overlook something, or if the AI adoptions slow down dramatically in the next few years, my bullish calls on NBIS and CRWV will likely age poorly.

Also, particularly for CRWV, the debt is a huge risk, although it's mainly secured by the backlog/contracts.

Despite the risks, unlike most NBIS long-term bulls whom I've read in the past few weeks, I'd pick CRWV over Nebius in the medium term. I don't have a strict PT in mind, but if the P/S ratio reprices higher to 6.5-7x again, we should see the current attempt of CRWV's stock price recovery keep going. The medium-term resistance level at $180-185/share gives an upside of over 30%:

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: PVH Corp.: Remain Cautious On The Near-Term Uncertainties
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on PVH Corp. (NYSE:PVH) was a hold rating because the demand outlook was poor, dragged down by a poor macro backdrop and tariff headwinds. I am starting to see early but encouraging progress on brand execution, particularly at Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger, where product innovation and high-impact marketing are driving traction. Operational execution is also stabilizing, which eases some prior concerns. That said, near-term earnings still face pressure from higher tariffs, muted revenue growth, and unresolved geopolitical risks in China. Against this mix, I remain cautious on the near-term setup.

Positive signs

There are early but encouraging signs that PVH is beginning to execute its brand-building playbook more effectively, which, in my view, is a critical step toward stabilizing the business. The strategy is straightforward. PVH is going to focus on core product categories, drive innovation within hero franchises, and ramp up high-impact marketing. Encouragingly, these are starting to show up in the numbers.

If we look at Calvin Klein, the brand is now leaning into its core product categories like underwear and denim, and the spring campaign with Bad Bunny was a good signal to the market that this focus is turning into actions. Basically, the campaign featured superstar Bad Bunny to promote the "newness and innovation" in the brand's new men's Icon Cotton Stretch underwear line. Importantly, it worked. The launch of the new men's Icon Cotton Stretch franchise saw a 14% global sales increase in Q2 2025, which goes to show just how important having the right combination of product innovation and marketing can be to drive demand. Since this is something executed from within (i.e., a structural lever), PVH should be able to repeat this elsewhere to reaccelerate growth. Indeed, management is now extending this formula to the women's business, and if successful, we should see a similar dynamic playing out.

Shifting to Tommy Hilfiger, the efforts to make this brand more culturally connected with consumers are also working. Take, for instance, the collaborations with the F1: The Movie and the US SailGP racing team, which have helped to drive strong sell-through in featured collections and are resonating particularly well with younger consumers. In my view, building this brand heat is very important because it creates a pathway to recapture pricing power and capture more consumer mindshare. Together, more recurring demand, better pricing, and higher margins. As such, I am fully supportive of management making the decision to step up marketing investment (in 2H25), even at the expense of near-term margins.

What is equally important elsewhere is how PVH has managed to stabilize execution at Calvin Klein (which I previously noted as deeply disappointing). Mainly, the focus here is that PVH managed to work through the operational disruptions tied to establishing the global product hub in New York, and it seems like things are running really well since management has guided sequential improvements already delivered for Fall 2025.

Looking ahead to spring 2026, they have also locked in targeted going-in margin improvements and restored on-time delivery schedules. Essentially, for the upcoming Spring 2026 collection, products are scheduled to be delivered on time, and PVH has already finalized its costs and initial pricing to secure the profit margins it originally targeted. Both of these are very meaningful developments because they directly address concerns about management's ability to execute on the broader PVH+ Plan, and this should ease some of the overhang on the stock.

Finally, perhaps the more important development is that underlying demand trends are showing signs of life. Recapping some data from the recent quarter: Direct-to-consumer revenue, which was down 3% y/y in Q1 2025, improved sequentially each month in Q2 and ultimately finished flat on a constant currency basis. In the Americas, DTC moved from a mid-single-digit decline to flat, while Europe delivered its fourth straight quarter of store growth and a second consecutive season of low-single-digit growth in wholesale order books for spring 2026. Together, these signals suggest that PVH's internal initiatives are working, and if this trend continues, we could see a solid recovery coming ahead.

Tariffs and China

Regarding tariffs, management now estimates that the new rates are effectively doubling, raising the gross headwind for FY25 to ~$1.15 per share from $1.05 previously, and here is the worrisome part—the burden is heavily weighted to 2H25, with Q4 facing the sharpest hit as higher-rate inventory moves through the channel. Although mitigation efforts are underway, the reality is that they will only partially offset the blow this year, so the margin is going to get hit hard, which will drag down the headline numbers. Combining that with top-line growth momentum not being really strong (reported revenue growth of 4% in Q2 2025 was largely flattered by a 3% foreign exchange tailwind and wholesale timing shifts, leaving core constant-currency growth at just 1%), with a full-year guide of "flat to increase slightly" suggesting no demand inflection, near-term earnings could see a big hit before they grow again (from the positive initiatives above).

Another dynamic that has played out negatively is geopolitical risks, PVH's inclusion on China's "List of Unreliable Entities." While there have not been any negative measures imposed yet, one cannot deny that the risk is real and significant. For context, China represented 6% of revenue and a much larger 20% of EBIT in 2024, so any weakness here can really hurt the business. China could impose fines or restrictions that can really undermine profitability and sentiment.

Altogether, as much as I like the positive signs developing, the near-term margin weakness and China uncertainties are things I want more clarity on before turning bullish.

Valuation

I guess the market reacted really nicely to the positive developments—the share price went from low-$60s to $85 today, and so has forward PE, up from 5.6x to 7.2x. My view is that the multiple will stay at this level, hovering near the average of ~8x (3-year average), until the market gains clarity on tariff impact and China uncertainties. As we look through to 2026, I think the setup will get incrementally better as the positive developments (hopefully) start to have a visible P&L impact, and PVH will cycle through an easier comp in 2H26. China will remain an uncertain element, but if PVH can show that it can continue to pull the structural levers to drive growth, I'd say there is a good chance multiples will go above average. Until then, I am on hold.

Conclusion

Overall, I am reiterating a hold rating on PVH. The brand-building playbook is beginning to show results, and operational stabilization at Calvin Klein improves confidence in management's ability to execute the PVH+ Plan. Longer term, these structural levers should set the stage for recovery. However, the near term remains weighed down by tariff-driven margin pressure, slow revenue momentum, and material uncertainty tied to China exposure. With valuation already rerates closer to historical averages, I believe the stock stays range-bound until there is more clarity on these risks and a visible P&L impact from the turnaround.

data.title: I Am Not Concerned About The Job Losses In The ADP Report
data.description:
Powerful bull markets take events that are not consequential to the ongoing economic expansion in stride, as this one did yesterday when the federal government closed for business. It was expected, and it likely won’t last very long. The S&P 500 logged its 29th record close for the year and a new all-time high. We saw new leadership from the healthcare sector with the announcement of a deal between the Trump administration and Pfizer. The pharmaceutical company will have a three-year reprieve from tariffs in exchange for lowered drug prices on four of Pfizer’s products to be sold on a new direct-to-consumer platform, which will be called, to no surprise, TrumpRx.

The fact that we don’t have government economic data for now means we have to focus more intently on what is provided by the private sector. It wasn’t a good start with the ADP payroll report. According to their payroll data from some 25 million private sector workers, the economy lost 32k jobs in September, and the prior month was revised from a gain of 54k to a loss of 3k. This is the first time we have seen back-to-back losses in jobs since 2020, but the caveat here is that an annual recalibration of its methodology reduced September’s number by 43k.

Those on the bearish side of the aisle will use this employment report to support their view that the economy is cracking, but I don’t think it is as disappointing as the headline number appears. ADP uses a different methodology than the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) to determine the size of the workforce and job creation numbers. As a result, there is often a big difference from one month to the next in their estimates. It has averaged more than 60,000 per month in 2025. The message from the ADP report is that job growth has stalled, which is the same message we are getting from the BLS. I don’t think we are realizing job losses. If we were, we would see a sharp increase in weekly unemployment claims, but that is not happening. Last week, we were back down to just 218,000.

What this ADP report does is increase the probability of another 25 basis point rate cut by the Federal Reserve at the end of this month. That probability rose to 99.4% yesterday after the report’s release, which is music to this bull market’s ears.

data.title: Maersk - Playing The Long Game
data.description:
Portfolio Commentary

As of 10/1/2025, my cumulative return for my portfolio from inception is 115% and overall return year-to date is 13.2%.

Here is my current allocation as of 10/1/2025:

*This does not indicate other holdings are Buys in my opinion*

Since my last article in May 2025, I sold my Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) and American Express (AXP) positions to add more Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) and Bill Holdings (BILL). AXP at a 102% gain and Berkshire at a 35% gain.

(SPY) is up ~12% YTD. My portfolio is up ~15% YTD return.

Not many active managers are outperforming US indexes this year. And if they are outperforming US indexes, they are probably buying very over-valued companies.

My objective is to have gains of 15% every year and right now my annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) is 32% a year since inception.

I first invested in Maersk ADR shares in September 2024 when shares were below $8/share. I purchased many more shares in January, making Maersk 50% of my portfolio. My average cost for (OTCPK:AMKBY) is now $8.55.

The article below discusses one of my personal investments. I am not writing this because I am recommending you should buy the stock without further due diligence. Investing in single stocks is very risky. I recommend to everyone to buy SPY if you don’t want a financial advisor but want to invest in the stock market.

Maersk Overview

AP Moller-Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) was founded in Denmark in 1904, making it over 120 years old. Their core business is the Ocean segment (container shipping). The other two segments are Terminals (operating port infrastructure) and Logistics & Services (air freight, trucking/last mile delivery, and warehousing). Maersk is increasingly focused on becoming a global shipper of container logistics, aiming to manage the entire supply chain rather than just the ocean leg.

Before Covid, shipping was a cyclical business with modest returns. Maersk typically earned around $2-3 billion/year pre-pandemic.

Then Covid happened and bottlenecked the whole supply chain and the shipping companies made large amounts of money. Larger than usual.

Maersk generated a Net Income of $17.9 billion in 2021 and $29.2 billion in 2022—totaling over $47 billion in profit in just two years.

Now that spot rates have normalized profitability has compressed significantly. Maersk continues to be profitable through most rate environments.

Maersk trades for ~$30 billion today.

Maersk trades at 10x times or 10% yield 2018/2019 net income.

Maersk trades at 5x times, or 20% yield, 2024 net income.

Global Ocean Shipping Outlook

Many publicly-traded ocean freight companies are trading at or beneath the replacement value of their assets. This valuation implies the market believes these companies will lose money for the foreseeable future.

But who is going to ship goods across the seas if these companies fail? According to the UN Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD), approximately 80% to 90% of global trade by volume is transported by sea. It is the circulatory system of the global economy. Is there a different or cheaper way to transport millions of tons of goods to North America or Europe? The answer is no. Similar to railroads within the United States, containerships are the cheapest mode of transportation (by weight) across oceans.

The biggest narrative keeping the ocean shipping sector trading at single-digit PEs and low price-to-book ratios is the fear of massive oversupply and a subsequent collapse in spot rates.

This fear is not unfounded. During the COVID-19 pandemic, shipping companies generated unprecedented profits and reinvested heavily in new ships. This has resulted in a record-breaking "order book" (the pipeline of ships currently under construction). As of August 2025, the container ship orderbook reached a record high of 10.4 million TEU (twenty-foot equivalent units).

As shown in the chart below, Maersk is the second largest liner in the world by capacity/TEUs.

Maersk is the second-largest carrier in the world and the largest publicly traded container shipping company by capacity. This scale is crucial in a commoditized, high-fixed-cost industry.

Analysts are concerned that with more supply coming, demand will fall.

Current Rate Environment

As seen in the Containerized Freight Index above, ocean freight rates are continuing to fall. This index tracks the most popular trade lanes in ocean shipping and aggregates the rates into one figure. As you can see, thanks to COVID shutdowns in 2020, freight rates boomed to all time highs. As of late September 2025, rates are falling to 2 year lows and approaching pre-Covid levels.

Just as analysts projected, rates are declining.

This has lead to the recent price decline in Maersk's stock price as Maersk's profitability is directly impacted by freight rates.

Now that freight rates are at breakeven levels (or even below breakeven for some), what does that mean?

It's likely that most, if not all, ocean shipping companies will lose money in the short term as the price to ship a container falls below their cost.

And the larger players in the space most likely survive.

If you are the low-cost leader and well-capitalized, you should survive any downturn and gain market share.

The market is pricing in the worst-case scenario while ignoring the strategic shifts Maersk has made to navigate this environment.

Ocean Strategy: Reliability + Cost Leader with High Volume

In February 2025, Maersk launched the "Gemini Cooperation," a partnership with Hapag-Lloyd (the world's 5th largest carrier). The explicit goal of this alliance is to achieve schedule reliability above 90%. This is revolutionary in an industry where the norm, according to maritime data provider Sea-Intelligence, typically hovers around 50-60%.

The network uses large, efficient vessels on mainline routes that sail only between key transshipment hubs. Crucially, these hubs are largely owned or controlled by Maersk (through APM Terminals) or Hapag-Lloyd.

The results are already proving the concept. During the Q2 2025 earnings call, CEO Vincent Clerc reported that the network was achieving +90% reliability.

This partnership allows Maersk to pursue a high-volume, cost-leader strategy.

In Q2 2025, Maersk charged lower prices per TEU compared to major competitors. The Gemini Network increased their volume delivered.

When compared to these other publicly traded liners, in Q2 2025, Maersk had the highest volume at the lowest price.

This strategy is designed to gain market share by increasing capacity without buying more ships. During a downturn, offering a superior product at a competitive price should prove successful.

Contracts vs. Spot Revenue

Maersk has two buckets for bookings: spot (rates agreed upon at the time of transaction) and contract (rates locked in 6+ months in advance). Now management refers to these buckets as short-term vs long-term products.

Contract revenue uses future contracts traded in the market. This means today, 10/1/2025, there is a futures contract for one TEU delivery from China to the US West Coast for 12/1/2026 or 2/1/2026.

Offering customers long-term contracts provides the customer (like Walmart, Nike, or Home Depot) peace of mind against spot rate volatility and ensures Maersk maintains a base load of volume. When spot rates decline, contract revenue provides a buffer. The downside is that if spot rates rapidly increase, Maersk leaves money on the table.

Maersk has heavily favored this conservative approach in the past. In their most recent interim report (Q2 2025), they shared that their revenue was split 50/50 between contracts and spot.

Currently, all the freight futures for 2026 deliveries are above current spot rates.

Meaning, if Maersk leans more into long-term contracts, they will make more revenue than shipping companies delivering more at spot rates.

Another advantage for the large-volume player.

APM Terminals

Another aspect of Maersk that may not be appreciated by the market is their investments into ocean terminals.

Maersk has invested billions into port terminals that load and off-load containers from Maersk ships or competitors' ships. With the establishment of the Gemini Network, APM Terminal's revenue and profits continue to grow at a steady pace thanks to the higher volume strategy in the Ocean segment.

Last 12 months, the Terminals segment had earnings of $1.5B. Growing revenue 15-20% a year with $1.5B profit.

I could make the argument that the APM Terminals segment alone is worth $30B. And Maersk as a whole is trading for $30B.

$1.5 billion x 20 times multiple = $30 billion valuation

A Fortress Balance Sheet

The market is currently valuing Maersk around a $30 billion market cap as of September 2025.

Maersk has $88B of assets and $30 billion in TOTAL liabilities.

Book value, or replacement value, is what accounting says it would cost to recreate Maersk today—to build the modernized fleet, secure the highly coveted global terminal slots, and replicate their logistics network. Book value is almost double the market cap of Maersk shares.

As of the Q2 2025 report, Maersk reported net interest bearing debt of -$2.5 billion. This means Maersk is in a strong net cash position—they have cash of $19 billion and debt of $17 billion.

Most asset heavy businesses have high debt positions, like railroads, because of large amounts of fixed assets (ships, terminals, etc.). Maersk has $88 billion in assets.

The book value per share is ~DKK 23,000, while the stock is trading near DKK 12,500. It's almost like buying a dollar for 50 cents.

This is a margin of safety. If the company is unprofitable in the short-term, at least you bought the company at a price beneath Net Asset Value.

Share Buybacks

In February 2025, Maersk initiated a significant DKK 14.4 billion (~$2 billion USD) share buyback program.

Since the initiation, Maersk has bought 5.5% of $Maersk-B shares outstanding. That's a 5% yield to shareholders in half a year.

Then you factor in the price support.

Maersk releases weekly press releases announcing how many shares they bought in the week prior. This is not done by typical NYSE/American stocks.

These buybacks are making up a significant portion of the daily traded volume—often 15-25% of shares being sold—nearing the regulatory maximum.

The EU Commission and the SEC have laws against companies purchasing more than 25% of daily traded volume because companies could manipulate stock prices with these large purchases.

If Apple (AAPL) decided to buy 25% of their average daily traded volume, they would have enough cash to do it for ~18 days. This is the difference between a highly liquid stock and low liquidity stock like Maersk.

Because Maersk shares have little activity/selling, Maersk is approaching this "stock manipulation" threshold while trying to return only $2B of capital to shareholders over the course of a year.

The stock repurchase plan provides strong support for the share price and reduces the share count at prices well below book value...

With a net cash position, it's likely that Maersk announces another stock repurchase plan in February 2026.

Risks

No investment is without risk, and Maersk faces several significant challenges. Most of which have already been discussed.

Oversupply and Rate Collapse: This is the primary risk. If the delivery of the 10.4 million TEU order book overwhelms global demand, spot rates could fall below cash break-even levels for a prolonged period in which Maersk will be unprofitable or breakeven.

Normalization of Red Sea Routes: Currently, disruptions in the Red Sea are forcing ships to sail around the Cape of Good Hope. This longer route effectively absorbs excess capacity (estimated by analysts to be 7-10% of global capacity). If the Red Sea routes reopen safely, this capacity will be released back into the market, putting immediate downward pressure on rates.

Global Recession: Ocean freight is highly correlated with global GDP. A significant economic downturn would reduce the demand for goods, compounding the oversupply issue.

DKK currency risk: I invest in Maersk through the ADR (OTCPK:AMKBY). This means currency exchange rates between US dollars and Danish Krones affect the ADR share price. Since my initial investment, this has been a tailwind providing more gains. If the dollar strengthens more in the future, the ADR will be negatively affected. If the dollar continues to weaken, the ADR will be positively affected.

Conclusion

My thesis is that the market is overly focused on the near-term supply headwinds in the ocean shipping sector and underappreciating Maersk's strategic pivot towards reliability.

The ocean freight industry may have too many ships in the short term, but Maersk is exceptionally well-positioned to survive falling spot rates due to its strong balance sheet, contract-heavy revenue mix, and the efficiency and volume of the Gemini Cooperation.

In an industry becoming more commoditized, Maersk's strategy of providing low-cost leadership combined with premium reliability should increase market share and ensure long-term profitability. The current valuation provides a significant margin of safety.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Accenture: Short-Term Challenges, Long-Term Potential
data.description:
There is a huge difference between how we're all using AI in our individual lives that is incredibly easy and what it takes to use it in an enterprise.

The quote belongs to Julie Sweet, Accenture's (NYSE:ACN) CEO, and she said it during the conference call where they presented the Q4 and FY25 results. Sweet took a few minutes to explain in detail the way in which the company is expanding its revenue through customer demand to incorporate greater uses of AI across the business organization. In this context, she said the following, and I think it's wonderful because it also summarizes my impression of AI: it is a revolutionary technology, but generating profits above the invested capital isn't something as quick or simple as it may seem.

Similarly, Sweet highlighted the well-known topic of ROI, a topic recently addressed by MIT, which noted that only 5% reported achieving positive returns on investment.

It is well recognized that advanced AI has taken the mind share of CEOs, the C-suite and Boards faster than any technology development we've seen in the past 2 decades. At the same time, as reported widely, value realization has been underwhelming for many and enterprise adoption at scale is slow other than with digital natives. This is why our clients are turning to us. We know that the gap between mind share and faster actual adoption is because the enterprise reinvention required to truly unlock the value of advanced AI is hard and has significant costs.

Julie Sweet, CEO of Accenture

Investment Thesis

I wanted to start my analysis of ACN with this perspective from Sweet, which I found very interesting, regarding a current dilemma that some analysts, usually labeled as pessimistic, have been warning about. Wall Street is racing at a speed that can't withstand the entire race for mass euphoria over AI. Ultimately, AI's leadership will be seen in the long term, since, as Sweet said, we are in the first stage.

On July 18th, I wrote an article about ACN and its AI potential, rating it a buy after the company suffered a negative impact due to the DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) cuts and a 6% year-over-year decline in new booking revenue.

Interestingly, now that the company has regained momentum in new deals and posted a 6% YoY increase (from $20.15 billion to $21.31 billion) in Q4 FY25, the bearish blow came from a slightly lower-than-market-expected earnings projection for fiscal year 2026. The share price fell from $239 to $230 after the results were released, although it rebounded to $247 in the last week. Looking at the bigger picture, that is, looking at the year-to-date figures, ACN didn't have the best performance seen in previous years and was somewhat left behind compared to the big tech companies that are dominating the market: it fell almost 30%.

Be careful; if we expand the perspective a little further, for example, to 5 years, the yield is not the most attractive, with a total return for investors of 18%. Moreover, the recent lows in August and September have not been seen since March 2021.

Anyway, I think that ACN is playing a different game since the AI boom that started to explode strongly in 2022. I'm confident that ACN has several key strategies that have led them to their great potential. I will mention three, but there could be more:

  • Procurement policy: According to a report (slide 121) by the European Commission, ACN leads global acquisition activity with an average of 9.1 operations per year, above Microsoft's (MSFT) 6.7 and IBM's 6.1. Recently, the company acquired CyberCX, an Australian cybersecurity brand; IAMConcepts, a Canadian identity security firm; and Orlade, a French advisory services company.

  • Leading partners: ACN is working with the major companies that provide cloud services (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Oracle, etc.), and these account for 60% of their revenue.
  • Staff training: Based on what was said on the conference call, in fiscal year 2023 the company had 40,000 AI professionals and currently has 77,000, accounting for more than 6,000 advanced AI projects by 2025.

ACN has been investing in AI since 2019, and in recent years, we can see, in my opinion, that ACN has gained the potential to be worth more than it currently is on the market, moving toward a P/E ratio of 19x compared to the sector median of 30x or the historical average of 29x over the last five years. This implies an undervaluation of approximately 34% to 36%.

In my last article, I focused more on ACN's leadership in the AI sphere. But today I want to analyze in more detail, in addition to the FY25 results, the reason for a further drop in price and its earnings projection in parallel with the market outlook, concluding that I not only maintain my Buy rating (the price has fallen 12% since the publication of my previous article), but I also confirm that ACN is a great future opportunity despite the present risks.

Q4 FY25 and the Positive: Recovery of New Bookings

The news of the new contracts eased the bearish sentiment in a context where expectations about AI-generated revenue are the most closely watched issue among investors. In the quarter, new contracts totaled $21.31 billion, while in the fiscal year they totaled $80.62 billion, a slight 1% contraction compared to $81.23 billion in FY2024. The significant bearish sentiment weighing on the company in 2025, partly due to federal spending cuts, ultimately led to a slight decline of just 1%. At the same time, GenAI's bookings were also encouraging: they were almost double those of 2024, at $5.9 billion.

The other metrics, in general, are all positive and exceeded market consensus figures, such as the revenue of $17.6 billion (7% YoY), $236 million above expectations, or the adjusted EPS of $3.03, a surprise of $0.06 compared to the estimated EPS.

If we leave aside the quarterly results and focus on the fiscal year, we will also find positive numbers, generally speaking, despite the headwinds: revenues were $69.7 billion (+7% YoY), with the largest increase in the financial services sector (+10%), the adjusted operating margin was 15.6% (+10 bps), and annual net income closed at $7.8 billion, above the $7.4 billion expected in 2024.

Another example of the company's resilience is, in my view, the best performance of the year, which, in my opinion, is the free cash flow of $10.9 billion (+26% YoY), which allowed it to return $8.3 billion to shareholders, of which $4.6 billion went to share repurchases and the remainder to dividend payments, a solid and consistent cash flow that demonstrates the company's strong position.

FY25 and the Challenges: What's Causing the Decline?

We've already mentioned the cuts at AFS (Accenture Federal Services), and it's estimated there will still be some impact on revenue during 2026, as the company estimates growth of between 2% and 5%, assuming a 1% to 1.5% impact on AFS. Excluding the cuts at AFS, revenue profitability is close to the fiscal year 2025 yield of 7%, with a target of 6% at the most optimistic end.

Although it may seem like a logical doubt for many investors who view the 2026 estimate as a concern, the reality, from my perspective, is that this is a long-term opportunity because 1) there are no operational issues at the company and 2) the pessimism made the stock a cheap and attractive entry option. Furthermore, all other forecasts are solid, in my opinion, with the added benefit that shareholder returns continue to grow, according to estimates, to $9.3 billion.

On the other hand, there are other areas where the company faces challenges, one of which is employee training and talent turnover to optimize AI development. In this regard, the company reported a $615 million cost for business optimization, of which $344 million is for employee severance pay, reflected in the decrease in headcount, which fell from 790,000 in the previous quarter to 779,000. According to statements made during the call by CFO Angie Park, they plan to add an additional $250 million by Q1FY26 (Nov 2025), completing the process at a total cost of $865 million.

Another positive outlook for the future, rather than a challenge, is the steady growth of the company's investment in cybersecurity, one of the largest revenue drivers in recent years, especially thanks to the various acquisitions of smaller companies. Security is the fastest-growing job type in local currency in FY25, up 16% to $10 billion, with an estimated 9% CAGR through 2028.

Valuation

In my last article on the company, I developed a valuation model using multiples compared to the sector. However, this time, to understand the company's value relative to its earnings per share and the market outlook, I will perform a valuation based on GAAP EPS and GAAP P/E, metrics that are more conservative than non-GAAP and which I consider the basis of my calculations.

I want to clarify that I aim to be conservative to demonstrate that ACN only needs a modest amount of confidence to recover its recent historical prices. In this pursuit of conservatism, I am using the lower end of the GAAP EPS projection for FY2026, which is $13.19. I could use the midpoint estimate, $13.38, or even the upper end, $13.57, but I am opting for a conservative model.

I apply the same concept to the expected GAAP P/E, which could be 30x or even higher in an optimistic scenario. Below, I show the evolution of the P/E over the last 5 years.

Based, then, on a GAAP EPS of $13.19, I estimate conservative scenarios with a recovery of the GAAP P/E that, without reaching the historical average, would still generate attractive returns. The base price I use is $245.

Scenario EPS GAAP Estimate P/E GAAP Target Price Upside
Bearish $13.19 18x $237 -3.2%
Base $13.19 20x $264 +7.8%
Moderate $13.19 23x $303 +23.6%
Conservative $13.19 25x $329 +34.3%
Mildly bullish $13.19 27x $356 +45.1%

Pessimistic valuation scenarios, i.e., GAAP P/E below 18x, haven't been seen in the last 5 years, and we'd have to go back to 2017 to find a 16x ratio. That's why I used a floor of 18x, so I don't see much further downside. All other scenarios are bullish but moderate, and I consider the 25x scenario the most likely within a conservative scenario. In that case, the upside is 34.3%, and the target price is $329. I reiterate that I'm also using the lower estimate of GAAP EPS, and there's still room for upside.

Risks

The company's main risks are largely reflected in the challenges outlined throughout the article, although there are obviously other external risks that could end up impacting the outlook for a recovery in the stock price. Among these risks is US macroeconomic uncertainty, which, while pointing to activity growth for 2026, inflation and employment data are not looking very good in the eyes of the Federal Reserve. The recent rate cut and the projections of two more cuts by 2025 clear some doubts about activity, although it raises other concerns about inflation control.

On the other hand, ACN is also heavily exposed to international markets, as EMEA and Asia Pacific represent almost 50% of its revenue, which also entails foreign exchange risks. Added to this is the uncertainty surrounding potential labor restrictions on foreigners by the federal government, such as the H-1B visa measure and the $100,000 tariff. Sweet explained on the call that this will not have an impact on the company since only 5% of its staff has H-1B visas. ACN's largest concentration of staff is in India, with around 350,000 employees. By this, I mean that any new labor market measure that leads to cost overruns poses a risk to ACN under the Trump administration.

Conclusion

In short, the current challenges are convincing the market that the current value is a bargain compared to its potential and dispelling all shadows of future federal spending cuts. The opportunities, meanwhile, lie in the company's operational capabilities and its AI adoption strategy for its corporate clients. The added benefit is that the upside potential is high, and, in my opinion, this is a long-term investment.

data.title: DIAX: Merger On The Table, The Discount To NAV Could Shrink
data.description:
Thesis

We have covered the Nuveen Dow 30 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (DIAX) several times in the past on the Seeking Alpha platform. The name is a buy-write CEF from Nuveen that has poor structuring and poor long-term results. Our issue with DIAX has always been around its Dow Jones to S&P 500 basis, a structural item that we highlighted in our last article on the website.

Nuveen seems to be undergoing a streamlining of operations in the CEF space, with the recent merger of Nuveen Preferred Securities & Income Opportunities Fund (JPI) into the Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC), which was completed on September 22, 2025.

It seems like now the fund manager is looking at doing the same streamlining when it comes to its buy-write closed-end funds. In today's article, we are going to discuss the most recent corporate action and our view on the proposal.

What is happening?

The Board of Directors for several Nuveen CEFs has approved a potential corporate action:

CHICAGO, September 19, 2025--(BUSINESS WIRE)--The Boards of Trustees of Nuveen S&P 500 Buy-Write Income Fund (BXMX), Nuveen Dow 30℠ Dynamic Overwrite Fund (NYSE:DIAX), and Nuveen S&P 500 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (SPXX) have approved a proposal to merge the funds. The proposed mergers, if approved by shareholders, would combine BXMX and DIAX into SPXX. The mergers are intended to create a larger fund with lower net operating expenses and increased trading volume on the exchange for common shares.

Any merger in the CEF space starts with the Board of Trustees (Board of Directors). This approval starts the process, with the shareholders having the final say in the mergers.

The next step is for each CEF shareholders to vote on the proposed corporate action, with the outcome contingent on such a vote.

The Nuveen CEFs have not been the brightest stars

We have covered all the respective CEFs on the Seeking Alpha platform in the past, with the funds not being the brightest stars in the buy-write arena:

  • DIAX: The Basis Is Still There, Keep Avoiding This Buy-Write CEF.
  • BXMX: A Disappointing 2024 For This Equity CEF.
  • SPXX: Much Better Choices Out There.

The market has taken a similar view on the CEFs via the discount to NAV metric:

All the respective CEFs are trading at very deep discounts to NAV, all close to -10%. There is a whole new generation of ETFs and CEFs in the buy-write arena that have attracted significant capital and interest via their robust performance. Not for the Nuveen funds:

Please note that the Nuveen CEFs are at the bottom, all severely underperforming the S&P 500 (SPX) and other competitors in the space. In each dedicated article, we discussed the structural issues with each fund; thus, the above does not come as a surprise.

Our view on the corporate action

We are of the opinion that a consolidation is beneficial to the DIAX shareholders because it would remove the basis, although the fund mandate might change to follow the S&P 500. The asset manager is clearly trying to optimize and refocus the equities management team, and having one larger flagship fund is a better way to do it. Without any changes, DIAX will continue to underperform and be a forgotten CEF at a large discount to NAV. Posting a +39% total return when the SPX is up over +93% is not acceptable in our view.

We can see newcomers in the space like the Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI) garnering very significant interest and AUM due to their performance:

The market has already voted, and it has moved toward top performers who care about the underlying structure and relative returns versus an index. As a reminder, one would get an 11% yield via SPYI, and its performance in the past year is as follows:

While it is not entirely correct to benchmark DIAX to SPYI since DIAX is supposed to follow the Dow Jones, we can see the fund's significant lag to the Dow index itself in the above.

At the end of the day, investors are chasing performance in the buy-write space, and laggards will not attract AUM or interest. In fact, laggards end up trading at large discounts to NAV.

Closing of the discount to NAV—a potential +10% gain

A corporate action that would result in a larger, more streamlined fund with better structuring would probably also narrow down the discount to NAV. Currently, DIAX has a 9.6% discount to NAV, which could theoretically move to flat to NAV. We have seen these types of moves in the past when weaker funds were merged into better-structured funds. While nobody is guaranteeing any narrowing, a larger, more focused fund that would target better performance metrics could entice market participants to close some discount gap.

If the new entity performs better than its cohort or at least in line with peers, the market will reward it with a narrower discount. Thus, voting in favor of this corporate action can theoretically help performance via a lower discount to net asset value.

Conclusion

DIAX is a buy-write fund from Nuveen. The CEF has been plagued by structural issues and has underperformed. The market has 'rewarded' DIAX with a -10% discount to NAV, highlighting its issues. Nuveen is looking to streamline its buy-write CEF business via a merger that would see DIAX, SPXX, and BXMX combine. We view this as a positive outcome for the DIAX shareholders, which could see the massive -9% discount to NAV narrow to almost flat. We are in favor of the merger.
